coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted immense interest for theranostic applications. These applications of GQDs stem from chemical inertness, ultra-small size, and high aqueous dispersibility which probably enable their effective clearance without inflicting toxicity to the host. Relative to heavy metal-based quantum dots and organic fluorophores, GQDs display tunable bandgap and optoelectronic properties. Considerable efforts have been made to modulate their photoluminescence through doping of heteroatoms and controlling the size and edge profile. Subsequent changes in π-conjugation improve the luminescence stability, signal-to-noise ratio, and imaging depth unachievable with organic dyes. This review covers the synthesis and structural aspects of GQDs in context to their application in cancer diagnosis. After discussing the effect of chemistry (surface functionalization and doping) on optical properties, we present an inclusive view on the sensing of cancer-related biomarkers with GQDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Epoxy groups are produced over the carbon lattice during synthesis of GO sheets. Oxidative anions, derived from strong bases, act as ‘scissors’ and cut the sheets along epoxy groups.

Time scale changes in topography of graphene oxide sheets (A) as visualized by atomic force microscopy. Sheets were oxidized with sodium hypochlorite under UV radiation and images were acquired at 8 (B), 11 (C), and 13 (D) min under tapping mode. (scale bar: 500 nm).

Transmission electron microscopic image of GQDs synthesized in DMF (A1), DMSO (B1) and water (C1). Histograms in the inset of A1, B1 and C1 signify size distribution of GQDs. PL spectra of respective samples are displayed in the lower panel (A2, B2 and C2). Samples showed emission in blue-green regions. Each spectrum fitted with four Gaussians peaks (P1, P2, P3, P4) and intensity of these peaks differed depending on the synthesis process. The inset in A2 shows digital photograph of GQD aqueous solution excited with 312 nm.

Effect of electrolyte strength upon physicochemical characteristics of GQDs. Raman spectra of graphene paper (anode) after electrochemical oxidation-cutting process in NaOH and ammonia solutions. High I
D
/I
G ratio suggests superior oxidation and cutting of the paper in ammonia solution (A). This difference can be clearly noticed in TEM images (B-C). Graphene paper rapidly broke apart from the anode (termination of the electrochemical redox reaction) in case of NaOH. On the other hand, GQDs were produced in high yield through prolonged oxidation and efficient cutting of graphene paper in ammonia solution occurred (D-E).

Schematic representation of a typical laser ablation setup (A). The beam is directed through a prism and focused onto the sample using a lens. As the laser interacts with precursor, dense plasma of fragments develops and expands. GQDs are formed during cooling of plasma by the surrounding liquid. The process differs mechanistically in case of multi-walled CNTs as precursor (B). Here, outer walls of MWCNTs are first exfoliated to GQDs while inner walls remain intact. With further irradiation, the tubular structure of CNTs is completely transformed to GQD colloid.

Synthesis of GQDs with hydroxyl groups via controlled condensation of pyrene. Its aqueous dispersion emitted green fluorescence upon irradiation by a UV light (QY: 23%, A). Average thickness of QDs (1.47 ± 0.86 nm) corresponds to five layers (B). TEM image shows that the GQDs are well dispersed (C, average lateral size: 3.5 ± 0.6 nm, scale bar, 20 nm). Real-space image and its fast Fourier transform pattern (inset in D) show almost defect-free single crystals (D, scale bar: 1 nm). Adapted from Reference [130].

PL spectra of GQDs (A), PEI1800-GQDs (B), and PEI600-GQDs (C) at different excitation wavelengths. The insets in A, B and C show photograph of their aqueous solution under room light (left) and 365 nm UV irradiation lamp (right). Charge density graphs of carboxyl-GQD (D) and GQD-PEI (E). The carboxyl group shares the charge completely with the inner graphene core. In case of GQD-PEI, electrons are concentrated on the center of inner core suggesting that the conjugation domain was narrowed in the presence of PEI.

Reaction scheme showing GQDs synthesized in water, DMF and DMSO. Solvothermal reaction helps in the conversion of non-radiative –COOH and C-O-C groups into radiative carbonyl (CO) groups in N-GQDs and S-GQDs (A). In S-GQDs, SO groups reduce the effective n-π * transitions (B). PL spectra of undoped and doped GQDs are presented in (C). N-GQDs with an emission peak at 443 nm show ~25 times stronger PL intensity than that of S-GQDs and the S-GQDs exhibit ~2 times higher PL intensity (at ~454 nm) than that of GQDs.

Confocal laser scanning microscopic observations of HeLa cells treated with amine functionalized (NH-GQDs) and TAT-peptide functionalized GQDs (TAT-NGs). The mass ratio between acetylated-TAT peptides and NH-GQDs was set to 1:1, 5:1, and 10:1 during functionalization. After 24 h incubation, the NH-GQDs and TAT-NGs (1:1) were mostly located in the cytoplasm (A, B). TAT-NGs (5:1 and 10:1) crossed the nuclear membrane and located in the nucleoplasm (C, D).

T1-weighted MRI of A549 tumor-bearing nude mice prior to and post-injection of PGQD-HA. Formulation was injected to the animal via tail vein two hours prior to imaging. Tumor fringe can be clearly traced within 30 min of injection. The bright field gradually permeated to center until the tumor was entirely captured (A). Intra-tumor localization of folic acid-functionalized GQDs is shown in (B). In this case, the formulation was injected subcutaneously. The region of interest is highlighted with a circle.

Scheme for the preparation of proposed ECL immunosensor. Hydrazine modified GQD-Au NPs were dried over the glass carbon electrode. After removing unbound NPs, anti-CEA antibodies were drop-casted. The surface was blocked with bovine serum albumin (BSA) in order to avoid non-specific adsorption.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted immense interest for theranostic applications. These applications of GQDs stem from chemical inertness, ultra-small size, and high aqueous dispersibility which probably enable their effective clearance without inflicting toxicity to the host. Relative to heavy metal-based quantum dots and organic fluorophores, GQDs display tunable bandgap and optoelectronic properties. Considerable efforts have been made to modulate their photoluminescence through doping of heteroatoms and controlling the size and edge profile. Subsequent changes in π-conjugation improve the luminescence stability, signal-to-noise ratio, and imaging depth unachievable with organic dyes. This review covers the synthesis and structural aspects of GQDs in context to their application in cancer diagnosis. After discussing the effect of chemistry (surface functionalization and doping) on optical properties, we present an inclusive view on the sensing of cancer-related biomarkers with GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Early detection of cancer, prior to mass formation, is challenging but necessary to improve the survival rate of patients [1]. In a pre-operative and post-surgery follow-up, early detection offers the physician a time-span for contemplating the possible interventions well before the lesions become invasive. In spite of available screening techniques, the majority of patients are diagnosed after the disease has progressed to an intermediate or advanced stage [2]. This remains the leading cause of cancer-related mortality, particularly in low-income countries [3]. Fluorescence-based imaging remains a popular choice among surgeons for the diagnosis of cancer. The technique has evolved alongside the development of highly sensitive cameras which offer high spatial resolution [4]. A number of organic, inorganic and hybrid fluorophores have been described by research groups [5,6]. Organic fluorescent probes are excitable in wavelength region spanning from UV–visible to near-infrared (NIR) of the spectrum. UV-excitable fluorophores are harmful to living cells and hence, are not used in bioimaging [7]. On the contrary, NIR radiations exhibit deeper penetration without causing damage to biomolecules. As a result, indocyanine green (a NIR fluorophore) has been approved by US-FDA and EMA for clinical use [8].

In order to achieve high-affinity binding with the target, the fluorophore can be linked covalently on the polymeric backbone in conjunction with a recognizable molecule [9]. This allows direct visualization of biological analytes at a molecular level which can later be delineated in terms of cellular events. At the same time, these are helpful in the elucidation of biological phenomena at cellular and sub-cellular levels [10,11]. High imaging contrast can be achieved by cutting off tissue autofluorescence arising from non-target molecules [12,13]. However, the application of organic fluorophores is limited by photo-instability, pH and ionic sensitivity, susceptibility to photobleaching, chemical degradation, low permeability, and low quantum yield. Moreover, the interpretations are obscured by the broad overlap in the absorption and emission spectra [14,15].

Inorganic fluorophores exhibit large effective Stokes shifts (>80 nm), and therefore avoid self-quenching and light scattering during tissue imaging [16,17]. These are largely dominated by quantum dots (QDs) and semiconductor nanocrystals developed from elements such as cadmium (Cd), selenium (Se), tellurium (Te), and other heavy metals [18–20]. Optoelectronic properties of core-forming QDs can be improved by encapsulating with zinc sulfide (ZnS) shell [21]. QDs functionalized with immunoglobulin G and streptavidin have been successfully tested to label the Her2 on the surface of fixed and live breast cancer cells. The signals were target-specific and exhibited greater photostability in comparison to organic dyes [22]. Recently, Liu and co-workers [23] reported immunostimulatory action of CdSe/ZnS QDs in C57BL/6J mice. QDs acted as fluorescent nanoprobe, template for loading vaccine components, and inflammasome activator. While high loading of antigens corresponded to amplified innate and adaptive immune response, QDs enabled spatiotemporal tracking of lymphatic drainage.

In spite of interesting reports described above, in vivo applications of semiconductor QDs have been questioned on the account of poor biocompatibility, accumulation in highly perfused organs, and systemic toxicity. Besides, mechanistic aspects of QD toxicity have not been completely understood. Experiments on cell lines and microorganisms highlight the toxicity of QDs at structural and functional levels. It originates from the deregulation of intracellular calcium ions, development of oxidative stress, DNA impairment, mitochondrial destabilization, and autophagy induction [24–27]. Toxicity has been linked with the particulate properties [28], surface chemistry [29] and release of metals [25] from QDs. The research of Hu et al. [29] illustrated higher toxicity of small-sized, negatively charged CdSe/ZnS QDs in Phanerochaete chrysosporium cells. This can probably be the basis of their antimicrobial and anti-biofilm effects which can further be potentiated with the inclusion of antimicrobial peptides [30] or antibiotics [31].

The results on the use of organic coatings for improving the biocompatibility of QDs remain inconclusive. While some groups have reported low toxicity of capped QDs [10], others have noticed negligible effect. In one such study, PEGylated QDs caused injury in vital organs (liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) following acute and long-term exposure in mice [32]. In liver cells, QDs activate the NLR pyrin domain containing 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome thus leading to pyroptosis, a pro-inflammatory form of cell death [26]. Considering the toxicological profile of semi-conductor QDs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been suggested as an alternative.

Carbon, one of the most common elements in the human body, is a safer alternative [33,34]. Many researchers, including our group [35–38] have explored carbon-based nanomaterials for drug delivery and diagnostic applications. Such materials include fullerenes (zero-dimensional), carbon nanotubes (CNTs, one dimensional), graphene nanosheets (GNs, two dimensional), and GQDs (zero-dimensional). Physiological and immunomodulatory effects of carbon nanomaterials have been investigated at a greater depth [39] and have been described in earlier reviews [34,40–43]. Fullerenes, CNTs and GNs tend to aggregate quickly on the account of their hydrophobicity and dimension. Limited fluorescence of fullerenes hinders their utility in bioimaging [44]. CNTs show toxicity due to existence of metal impurities [45]. On the contrary, it has been shown through studies in small animals that GQDs are excreted harmlessly [46].

GQDs (lateral dimension <100 nm) exhibit tunable physicochemical properties and intrinsic photoluminescence (PL) relevant for bioimaging application. These can be synthesized from low-cost organic precursors with high-product yield [47,48]. Structurally, GQDs consist of a few layers (<10) of graphene fragments which exhibit a honeycomb pattern (sp2-sp2 carbon bonds) and absorbance in the UV region (230–300 nm) [49,50]. GQDs are regarded as superior carrier among carbon nanomaterials owing to their better solution processability, quantum confinement, and edge effects [51,52]. Quantum confinement enables widening of bandgap (from zero eV to that of benzene) as the material turns smaller [53]. It thus follows that PL can be controlled intrinsically by changing the lateral size of GQDs and inducing edge effects [52,54–56]. For example, GQDs containing high density of armchair edges and oxygen defects (epoxy and carboxylic acid groups) exhibit high PL quantum yield in comparison to those carrying zig-zag edges [57]. Surface functional groups and abundant edge sites allow the attachment of multiple copies of targeting moieties [16] useful for focussed bioimaging of a selective target. Nevertheless, passivation of oxygen defects during functionalization leads to suppression of non-radiative process (defect emission) while preserving the intrinsic state emission originating from the π-electron system of pristine GQDs [7]. It is noteworthy that the conjugated surface allows loading of drug molecules via non-covalent π-π stacking.

Applications of graphene materials in the fields of solar energy conversion, energy storage, and environmental remediation have been reviewed by Ma et al. [58] and Khan and co-workers [59]. This review encompasses synthetic approaches alongside the physicochemical properties of GQDs. An emphasis is placed on describing how the PL behavior and quantum yield of GQDs can be modulated through changes in the particle properties and inclusion of heteroatoms. Using up-to-date research findings, applications of GQDs in contrast-enhanced bioimaging, in situ diagnosis, and immunostaining of cancer-specific biomarkers have been discussed.

Synthetic methods have broadly been classified into top-down and bottom-up. General aspects of these approaches can be found in earlier reviews [37,60] and hence, these will be discussed specifically in context to GQDs synthesis. The specific method is selected based on the product properties, scale of production, and process yield. Presently, many efforts are directed towards the direct preparation of GQDs by one-pot synthesis.

Top-down approach leans over exfoliation of graphite or other carbon material into single- or few-layered graphene sheets. Later, sheets are broken up (carbon–carbon cleavage) in both dimensions via oxidation, solvothermal, electrochemical scissoring, and microwave treatments for obtaining GQDs with desirable particle properties. Restoration of π-conjugation in as produced GQDs occurs via in situ re-aromatization process [38,48,61].

Oxidative cleavage can be implemented with a simple instrumental setup. The cleavage is facilitated by strong oxidizing agents (H2SO4, HNO3, NaClO3, KMnO4, etc.) used under aggressive experimental conditions. The method exploits multiplicity of structural defects (epoxy and carboxyl groups) formed at the basal plane of graphene sheets during exfoliation [38]. Computational studies have implicated epoxy pairs, an intermediate species, which are aligned to produce a line defect at sheet surface ( Fig. 1). It may weaken the fracture stress of sheet up to 16% [62] thus triggering the breakage of C-C bonds under acid attack [63,64]. It must be mentioned that the edges remain unaffected because of being laden with carboxylic acid groups. Simultaneously, low value of fracture stress ensures that sheets do not undergo spontaneous breakage [63]. Smoothly-edged GQDs can be achieved by modulating the kinetics of acid attack.

The efficiency of oxidation process can be improved by irradiating the sample with UV light. Zhou et al. [66] demonstrated that UV rays accelerate the production of free radicals which subsequently bring about cleavage of C-C bonds. As the reaction time progressed from 0 to 13 min, the basal plane was ruptured with the formation of small pores ( Fig. 2). Consequently, micro-sized sheets were converted to single-layered QDs with lateral size 20–40 nm. This fragmentation can be verified from mass spectrometric data of as prepared GQDs. Sarkar et al. [67] observed repeating unit of m/z = 74 in between m/z of 1000–1600. The authors assumed m/z = 74 as the mass of six-membered carbon ring bonded to an armchair edge of GQDs. Here, the mass loss occurred because of the disappearance of two coordinate six-member carbon ring (MW: 76) and replacement by two hydrogen atoms (MW: 2). Loss of hydroxyl group (m/z: 16) from GQDs was repeatedly noticed between the peaks located at 1062 & 1078, 1136 & 1152, 1210 & 1226 and 1284 & 1300. One hydroxyl group was replaced by a hydrogen atom.

Additional steps can be included to obtain ultrafine particles and restore the original sp2 hybridization. The latter is accomplished by the treatment of QDs with reducing agents, such as L-ascorbic acid [68] and hydrazine [69].

Oxidative process is carried out in the presence of strong and corrosive acids. Destruction of CC bonds often accompanies with the generation of toxic gases (NO2 and N2O4), high amount of metal impurities, and other by-products of an unknown safety profile. More importantly, such impurities pose a considerable risk to the ecosystem [70]. These impurities must be eliminated through complex and time-consuming neutralization steps. Acid neutralization requires a strong base and results in the formation of salt in a large amount [71]. Another potential limitation is related to random cutting of sheets which damages the original graphitic domains and produces heterogeneously distributed particles [72,73]. Larger aggregates are eliminated by applying chromatographic and centrifugation techniques.

Solvothermal method involves solvent-assisted cutting of carbon materials under high temperature and pressure [74]. Given our focus on biomedical applications, we have limited our discussion on water as a green dispersion medium. Accordingly, the process termed as hydrothermal, is carried out in teflon-lined stainless steel reactors [75] available commercially in various capacities. Pre-oxidized graphene sheets or other carbon precursors are exposed to overheated steam under pressure. Such a treatment maintains the aromatic structure of carbon lattice by minimizing surface oxygen defects [65]. Hence, this method has been employed to regulate the density of oxygen defects in GQDs synthesized by other procedures. In such treatment, hydroxyl and epoxy groups are removed whereas carboxyl groups remain intact. This is attributed to the fact that hydroxyl and hydrogen radicals formed in the hydrothermal environment attack the oxygen defects to form water molecules. On the application point, this triggers a blue-shift, improves quantum yield, and tightens the full width at half maxima (FWHM) of fluorescence peak [76]. Notably, epoxy and hydroxyl groups act as non-radiative electron–hole recombination centers and therefore, suppress the fluorescence properties [77].

Properties of QDs can be optimized by controlling the temperature, pressure, and reaction time. Lu and co-workers [70] showed that reaction time can be minimized by including mild oxidizing reagents, such as hydrogen peroxide. Its dissociation into hydroxyl and oxygen radicals accelerates the breakage of a graphitic skeleton. Carbon atoms connected to hydroxyl and epoxide groups are attacked by hydroxyl radicals and C-C bonds are broken as a result. Later, newly formed oxygen-containing groups serve as new reaction sites [78]. Wang’s group [72] has shown that oxidation process can be advanced upon including FeCl3 in the reaction mixture. This was attributed to accelerated dissociation of H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals in the presence of Fe3+/Fe2+ ions. Given the ultra-small size of GQDs (2–5 nm), these could easily be recovered from the reaction medium since iron component precipitates as Fe2O3 at the prevailing temperature. Average yield, as calculated from twenty trials, was found approximately close to 60%. The technique of irradiating aqueous sheet dispersion with microwave has also been shown to improve the production yield [79]. Kellici et al. [80] introduced continuous hydrothermal flow synthesis (CHFS) which allows simultaneous and uninterrupted flow of supercritical water. It offered GQDs at an interval of less than one minute due to superior control over the hydrothermal treatment factors.

Some studies suggest that edge sites and functional groups in GQDs can be tuned by changing the reaction medium [57,81]. For instance, quantum dots synthesized in dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and water, displayed solvent-dependent PL spectra. Changes in spectral pattern correlated with the nature of edge sites and functional groups in GQDs. PL peaks marked at 405 nm (P1) and 440 nm (P2) were attributed to zigzag and armchair edge defects, respectively ( Fig. 3). Green emission bands at 490 nm (P3) and 530 nm (P4) originated from oxygen defects. In comparison to DMF, high concentration of zigzag edge sites were recorded in water [57].

In a typical electrochemical set-up, graphite electrode is cleaved into GQDs under high redox voltage [82]. The electrodes include carbon fibers grown on a carbon paper by chemical vapor deposition (CVD), petroleum coke–based electrodes made by calcination, and carbon paste electrodes prepared by packing method [83]. In the light of studies showing superior fluorescence profile of doped GQDs, researchers have tested hetero-atom doped electrodes for direct electrochemical exfoliation [83]. Among electrochemical bottom-up strategies, carbonization of low molecular weight alcohols has been successfully attempted for synthesizing amorphous GQDs. Identical UV–vis absorption was recorded for QDs prepared from propanol, butanol, ethylene glycol, and glycerol [84].

Electrolysis is carried out at room temperature and is less time-consuming in comparison to acid oxidation [85]. At the point of completion, GQDs can be recovered via solvent evaporation under vacuum. Mechanistically, the process can be divided into two stages: electrochemical fragmentation of graphite into graphene sheets, and electrolytic scission of sheets into GQDs [82,86]. As the reaction proceeds, the inter-sheet gallery expands thereby allowing the intercalation of more electrolyte species [87]. This can be verified from the conversion of colorless electrolyte solution to yellow and brown. Darker colors originated during the enlargement of sheets [88]. The size of as produced GQDs can be tuned with the parameters such as applied potential, oxidation and reduction time, and concentration of the supporting electrolyte [84,89]. Recently, Tian et al. [90] showed the superiority of alternating voltage over direct voltage in producing GQDs. The authors argued that positive voltage activates exfoliation process and oxidizes graphene sheets. Upon switching to a negative voltage, oxygen functional groups produced in the previous step undergo reduction. Thus, it helps in restoring the carbon-oxygen ratio of quantum dots. The technique is superior to one in which reduction is carried out by treating the electrolysis end product with hydrazine. Later, electrolyte and other impurities are removed by dialysis against deionized water [91,92].

Chemical reagents can completely be avoided by carrying out electrolysis in water as reported by Deng et al. [93]. The authors used two platinum sheets as electrodes and an aqueous dispersion of graphene oxide as electrolyte. Here, the applied voltage must be high enough to oxidize water and generate hydroxyl and oxygen radicals. An interesting aspect of using weaker electrolytes has been highlighted through a comparison between NaOH and ammonia [94]. The former, being a strong electrolyte, created harsh exfoliation conditions and triggered delamination of sheets instead of lateral cutting ( Fig. 4). Large sheets undergo quick re-stacking, a phenomenon that masks the electrochemically active surface [90]. Contrarily, prolonged oxidation interaction facilitates the continuous and efficient formation of GQDs.

Bahadur and coworkers [95] investigated the influence of sodium hydroxide concentration upon the properties of GQDs in citric acid-sodium hydroxide mixture as electrolyte. Reaction between the electrolyte components resulted into formation of sodium citrate salt and water. Alkali-derived hydroxyl ions intercalated through graphitic domains and produced QDs carrying oxygen groups in accordance to citric acid-sodium hydroxide molar ratio [95]. This means that the production yield of GQDs can be improved by increasing the concentration of active radicals. In separate experiments, Ma’s group [87] carried out electrolysis in the presence of H2O2 and UV radiation (18 W; 254 nm). The CC cleavage indeed was accelerated by as-produced free radicals and its effect resonated well into production yield. For instance, samples treated with H2O2 and UV offered 2.1 and 1.5-fold higher yields, respectively.

Microwave irradiation is attractive for GQD synthesis because of its uniform dielectric heating ability. The set-up includes a microwave equipped with an atmospheric reflux device. The reaction can be carried out in one-pot at ultra-fast speed (< 3 min) without negatively affecting the yield and product characteristics [96–99]. Similar to the hydrothermal process, it cleaves the carbon lattice along the epoxy defect line thus making the graphitic skeleton fragile. High product yield is ascribed to repetitive breakage of the skeleton through redox reactions. Accordingly, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic data of GQDs display a mixture of graphitic (CC and CC) and oxygenated carbons [48,53].

In the majority of top-down approaches, graphene sheets synthesized through acid oxidation are irradiated with microwaves. Exposure duration varies depending upon the lateral size of sheets. For example, large-sized sheets may need a longer exposure time [53,100]. This duration can be shortened with simultaneous application of ultrasound and microwave radiations. Nair et al. [101] obtained high-quality GQDs by sporadically irradiating the sample under sonochemical treatment. The synthesis was accomplished within 30 min and it offered a high product yield. With an increase in irradiation power from 100 to 400 W at a constant temperature of 90 °C, quantum yield increased from 9.2% to 23.8%. Recently, the effect of radiation power on GQD synthesis was investigated by Rasuli and coworkers [102]. GO sheets were employed as precursor. Radiation power was increased from 90 to 450 W maintaining the reaction time as 90 min. Oxidative functional groups disappeared moderately at low radiation power whereas breakage of sheets occurred at higher radiation power.
Apart from liquid-phase synthesis, solvent-free microwave-assisted methods have also been attempted by some groups [103,104]. Here, the precursor is converted to a fine powder before irradiation.

Few alternative top-down methods include microfluidization [107], electron-beam irradiation [108] and pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) [109]. The reader is referred to a recent review which describes the use of electron-beam irradiation technique for manipulating the material properties of carbon allotropes [110]. It has been shown that PLAL can transform carbon materials into GQDs within minutes even without using strong acids or bases. It employs fewer chemicals and offers product yields with few byproducts. Ablation mechanisms depend upon pulse duration. For example, femtosecond pulses transfer the energy to electrons of a substrate on a time-scale shorter than the electron–phonon thermal process. In the case of nanosecond pulses, the time scale is comparable with thermal relaxation process of the target. Therefore, it follows that thermal effects can be avoided with the use of short-duration pulses [111,112]. Breaking of CC bonds is facilitated either non-thermally by photon energy [113] or by pyrolysis and subsequent evaporation of the precursor [114,115].

Kang et al. [113,116] have reported the conversion of ethanolic dispersion of bulk carbon into GQDs under high-power sonication and laser ablation. Laser ablation was performed at room temperature using a Q-switch ND:YAG laser system. Authors suggested that a dense plasma of precursor fragments, ablated from the solid substrate during laser exposure, undergoes nucleation as GQDs while cooling inside the liquid. Thus, the method allows the easier inclusion of dopants from the dispersion medium [105,117]. GQDs are formed much promptly from precursors like CNTs ( Fig. 5). Black suspension of multi-walled CNTs turned into a transparent colloidal solution within 6 min [106].

Solution-phase exfoliation methods have been criticized for producing irregularly sized and shaped graphene sheets. This happens due to intrinsic randomness in the fragmentation pattern of the precursor. Such sheets tend to produce QDs with irregular edge profile and randomly distributed chemical groups. Optical properties of these dots are dominated by defect states instead of intrinsic confined states of nanographene [61]. A large number of sheets precipitate in dispersion medium which brings down the production yield to less than 10% [79,118]. Moreover, graphene oxide, carbon nanofibers, and other such precursors make the process expensive.

Taking into account the fundamentals of crystal growth, it has been argued that thermodynamically driven fabrication processes are more suitable than defect-mediated fragmentation [119]. These aspects can partly be addressed using a bottom-up strategy. Here, specific morphology and structures are created through a chemical reaction with precursors or assembly of small building blocks [120]. It employs low cost and environmentally friendly small organic molecules such as citric acid [121,122], cholesterol [123], glucose [124,125] and starch [126] for GQD synthesis. Essentially, GQDs are synthesized via stepwise condensation of solubilized precursors or reduction of pre-synthesized polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [82,127,128]. In addition to high productivity, bottom-up methods allow superior control over the size and morphology. High brightness and single-photon emission can be achieved from GQDs with appropriately functionalized edges [61].

Carbonization can be induced through microwave exposure [129], pyrolysis under reduced pressure [114] and hydrothermal treatment [130,131]. GQDs synthesis via pyrolysis of natural and synthetic molecules has been reported [114,132]. Citric acid (CA) and glucose are among the most widely studied small molecules [132–134]. Dong et al. [132] reported two stage procedure for the pyrolysis. CA was first melted under direct heating at 200 °C and later, treated with NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. GQDs were obtained after neutralization of NaOH. Growth of QD islands and density of sp2 clusters can be modulated by the extent of pyrolysis. It can possibly be related to the fact that CA molecules first condense into sp2 nuclei which is followed by crosslinking and stacking. At a fixed temperature of 200 °C, the population of initially produced GQDs (0.7–1 nm) started declining after peaking at 30 min. GQDs could not be detected after a reaction time of 60 min [135]. This could be assigned to protracted carbonization and, possibly, transformation into GO sheets instead of quantum dots [136]. Pyrolysis of macromolecular precursors is slightly complex as it first undergoes dissociation into simpler molecules. For example, starch is hydrolyzed into glucose, fructose, maltose, and aldehydes. Similarly, chitosan undergoes de-polymerization into pyranose rings which are later decomposed through dehydration and deamination [131]. As glucose molecules undergo dehydration, hydrogen atoms interact with hydroxyl groups of an adjacent glucose molecule, and formyl groups react with hydroxyl groups. Carbon atoms covalently interact with each other to form aromatic rings of graphene structure [126].

Thermal decomposition of silicon carbide (SiC) is a well-tested approach for the synthesis of single-layer wafer-size graphene [137]. Lately, it is being explored for GQD synthesis. Probably, it exploits the fact that the sheets become discontinuous during vacuum annealing at high temperatures and form small (diameter: 30–100 nm) domains [137,138]. Lee et al. [139] performed vacuum annealing of flat silicon carbide (SiC) plates at 1500°C in an argon-hydrogen (96:04 by weight) environment. As a result of etching, the surface turned bumpy onto which hydrogen-terminated highly ordered GQDs were deposited. Edge-functionalization with alcoholic groups was achieved through sonication in ethanol [139].

Precursors consisting of carbon skeleton similar to that of graphene’s primitive cell undergo molecular fusion under hydrothermal conditions. The reaction is performed in environmentally friendly polar solvents. The work of Wang et al. [130] highlights this concept using pyrene derivatives which carry four peri-fused benzene rings. Single-crystalline GQDs were produced through controlled condensation of pyrene and 1,3,6-trinitropyrene. With its three positively charged sites, nitrated pyrene molecules fused into GQDs during hydrothermal treatment in alkaline aqueous condition. Edge-site functionalization prevented sp3 defects within the basal plane and imparted high aqueous dispersibility (2000 mg/L) upon re-dispersion ( Fig. 6, A–D). Later, it was shown that the bandgap of QDs could be manipulated over 2.15–2.89 eV through changes in the composition of reaction solvents. Narrow- and wide-bandgap QDs were, respectively, developed using a single solvent (DMF, ethanol) and their mixture with auxiliary solvents, such as acetic acid and water [140].

Fullerenes are zero-dimensional caged structures composed of 60 (C60), 70 (C70), or more carbon atoms packed into fused hexagons and pentagons [119,141]. On the account of relatively low cost among fullerenes, C60 molecules have been widely used for synthesizing graphene materials of a diverse size range. It remains stable at room temperature but undergoes fragmentation upon annealing beyond 700 °C. The build-up of layers on these fragments can be minimized by facilitating the decomposition in low-temperature and ultra-high vacuum environment. Such a condition is favourable for GQD synthesis as it lowers the solubility of carbon and prevents its diffusion into the metal substrate [142].

Cage-opening and fragmentation of fullerene C60 into graphene structures has been reported on Ni [143], Ir [144], Cu [145] and other metal surfaces [119]. The binding energy of the C60-metal interface differs among these metals and, accordingly, interfaces are classified as strongly or weakly interacting. For example, C60-Ir exhibits strong interfacial interaction as a result of charge transfer from Ir (111) surface to electronegative C60 molecule. Strong interactions enable spontaneous decomposition of C60 to form graphene at elevated temperature [144]. These interactions are weaker for metals such as copper wherein the unzipping of C60 is impeded by its molecular polymerization [142]. Thus, the synthesis of GQDs demands the maintenance of strict work-up conditions and sophisticated instrumentation. As an alternative, classical Hummers’ method has been used by some groups for cage opening and fragmentation of fullerenes [146–148]. However, as highlighted in previous sections, its large-scale application is limited by poor reproducibility and inferior quality of as produced GQDs.

In recent years, green synthesis has grabbed significant attention. Precursors derived from biomass, such as vegetable waste, dead leaves, crops and other organic materials render the process cost-effective and less hazardous to the environment. The strategy allows better utilization of these resources. More importantly, it minimizes the pollution caused during their conventional disposal through burning and land-filling [149–153]. Typically, it involves synthesis of carbon flakes or graphene sheets (bottom-up) which are later converted to GQDs using top-down approaches described earlier. The first step is generic and can be applied to most of the available dry biomass [149]. Contrarily, finer organic materials can be converted into quantum dots in just one step as demonstrated by Mohammed and coworkers [154]. Although the biomass is a renewable, its careful processing is required while converting it as ready-to-use precursor material [155]. In addition, the methods of producing sheet precursors must be improved in terms of number of layers [156]. The readers are referred to a recent review by Espro and co-workers [157] on GQDs obtained via green synthesis.

Bioimaging applications have emerged from the fascinating optical properties of GQDs. Unlike quantum dots produced from group IV-VI elements of the periodic table, optical transition in GQDs can cover UV–visible and NIR regions of solar spectrum [158]. Its absorption spectrum shows a strong (λmax: 240–290 nm) and weak peak (λmax>300 nm) arising from π–π * and n–π * transitions, respectively [159,160]. PL varies in accordance with the density of delocalized π electrons at the basal plane and surface defects. It thus puts into focus the role of electron-withdrawing and electron-donating groups in modulating the charge transfer effect. It is possible to tune the emission maximum by the interplay of lateral size, edge profile, and the type of functional groups. For example, quantum confinement and edge effects induce PL when the size of the dot is less than 10 nm [161]. This facilitates multichannel detection and multicolored imaging at a high signal to noise ratio [162].

The emission peaks arise from electronic transitions between HOMO-LUMO levels of the highly conjugated basal plane. Theoretical and experimental findings unequivocally suggest that the energy level of these orbitals changes in the presence of additional functional groups [163]. Oxygen functional groups perturb the electronic structure of GQDs by destroying local π-electron density. This is ascribed to changes in the hybridization of carbon following its σ bonding with oxygen groups. Aside from triggering asymmetric variations in electron structure, such groups act as non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers [164]. Therefore, functionalization of basal plane is considered an important tool for enriching the electron density. It has been shown that reduction of carbonyl, epoxy, and amide moieties into hydroxyl groups indeed suppresses non-radiative processes and restores the integrity of π-conjugated system [7,38,165]. In the following section, we have reviewed the strategies of improving the optical behavior of GQDs through surface functionalization and doping with heteroatoms.

Carboxyl and hydroxyl groups serve as chemical handles during the functionalization of GQDs. Non-radiative processes associated with rotation and vibration of these groups can simply be minimized by esterification with small organic molecules [166]. At the same time, these enable adsorption of polymers and charged components through electrostatic, and a variety of non-specific interactive forces. The latter are dominated by π-π stacking interaction with molecules carrying aromatic centers, such as porphyrins and phthalocyanines. Non-covalent combination of GQDs with these macrocycles is accompanied by fast electron transfer without affecting the structural integrity of participants [167,168]. Such interactions are attractive from one more viewpoint, as revealed in the study of Cao et al. [40]. Optical properties of GQDs were coupled with the singlet oxygen generation ability of porphyrin derivatives for developing an advanced photothermal-photodynamic platform [40]. In the presence of targeting moieties, such formulations are translocated to cytosolic targets and offer superior therapeutic outcomes against cancerous growth [169]. Other functionalization strategies are aimed at improving the solvent affinity [169,170] and optical properties of GQDs. For example, electron-withdrawing fluorine atoms impart photostability against pH variations in a complex biological environment [79].

It is worth mentioning that changes in the emission profile of GQDs may occur in accordance with the molecular size of interacting residues. This is minimal (red-shift <10 nm) for short peptides [169] but can lead to complete white light emission with sterically bulkier groups [170]. Molecular basis of these events has been elucidated by Liu’s group [162]. Authors developed blue-, yellow-, and red-emitting GQDs by coating with polyethyleneimine (PEI) of different molecular weights (600 and 1800 Da). Given that the amide linkage between GQDs and PEI was established through engagement between carboxyl and amine groups, accompanying changes in electron density were investigated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Following linkage of PEI-1800, electrons were concentrated on the center of the inner graphene core ( Fig. 7). The resultant narrowing of the conjugation domain enlarged the bandgap and triggered high energy (blue-shifted) emissions from a single GQD core. On the other hand, the conjugation area was enlarged during the connection between multiple GQDs cores inside PEI-600 cage and red-emission followed.

Optical properties of GQDs are potentially deteriorated by the structural defects emanating from C-O-C and COOH functional groups. These groups trigger non-radiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs unconducive to PL [7]. Non-radiative processes can be suppressed through the doping of heteroatoms (nitrogen, boron, phosphorus, and sulfur alone and/or in combination). The energy difference between low-lying energy states becomes lower in the presence of dopants. Resultant changes in electronic structures favor the radiative transitions and thus, enhance the quantum yield. In such cases, the specific nature of spectral shift is dictated by the hybridization of frontier orbitals [171–173]. The process of doping is simple as the dopants can be introduced during the synthesis of GQDs. For instance, nitrogen and boron can be doped employing urea and boric acid, respectively, as commonly available raw materials. Multiple heteroatoms have also been doped in one step with the use of ionic liquids [174]. Specific ionic liquid can be selected to meet the elemental requirement. Nitrogen (N) and boron (B) doping have been examined with greater interest because of their similarity to carbon in terms of atomic radius. Five valence electrons of N can bond with carbon atoms and, thus offer a high quantum yield [52, 176–178]. Moreover, various configurations of C-N (pyrrolic-, graphitic-, pyridinic-N) affect the electronic state of GQDs and contribute to N-dependent luminescence [121]. Doping of larger elements, sulphur for instance, into graphene’s skeleton is challenging on the account of mismatch between CC and CS bond length. The latter is 25% larger than the CC bond [179,180]. These factors affect the overall charge density of sp2 carbon atoms. Das et al. [175] compared the optical properties S and N-doped GQDs obtained from graphene oxide sheets. QDs were synthesized via solvothermal treatment using DMSO and DMF as media. As a result of its electron releasing characteristics, nitrogen could mask the non-radiative traps and provide higher quantum yield. Contrarily, in the presence of electron-withdrawing SO functional groups, CS/CS bonds could not enhance the electronic charge in sp2 carbon and the band-gap increased as a result ( Fig. 8).

For a specific dopant, electronic transitions are significantly affected by its doping configuration. A DFT analysis on three configurations of boron (BC3, BC2O, and BCO2) showed that the configuration BC3 determined the electronic transitions. The effects of BC2O and BCO2 on electronic and optical properties were dictated by their hybridization form [178]. On a similar line, experimental investigations on N-doped GQDs showed higher PL intensity originating from areas with a greater percentage of pyridinic-N and a lower percentage of pyrrolic-N. This was attributed to comparatively greater contribution of delocalized π electrons from pyridinic-N [121].

A combination of hetero-atoms affords additional advantages. This includes an extension of absorption band into visible region [181] and attainment of two-photon excitation properties. The latter arises from the coexisting electron-withdrawing and donating sites. Microscopic techniques based on two-photon fluorescence can reveal the biological events inside deep tissues with greater resolution [171]. Unlike carbon-based fluorescent materials, co-doped GQDs exhibit excitation-wavelength independent emission and also single exponential function in PL lifetime decay. This can be interpreted as luminescence emerging from discrete emissive sites, apparently from graphene core [181]. At certain vacancy defects, co-doped GQDs may exhibit red-shift to NIR-II region (maximum emission > 1000 nm). The ability of QDs to absorb NIR radiation and convert it into heat can be exploited for the ablation of cancer cells. Interestingly, such QDs exhibit excellent photostability in physiological media [182]. Besides, co-doped GQDs exhibit superior performance in catalysis and energy applications [174].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of GQDs in diagnosis of cancer
ce:section.ce:para: High hydrophilicity, chemical inertness, and optical properties of GQDs have been exploited for applications ranging from imaging of tissues to the detection of biomolecular changes in cells. Detection of aberrantly-dividing cells in a pre-invasive state can be a potential breakthrough in improving the survival rate of patients. Lately, their quenching behaviour has been researched in context to the detection of drugs and metabolites in biological matrices. Such applications have been described in the proceeding section. Biological applications, however, demand comprehensive proof of the safety of GQDs. Hitherto, biosafety of GQDs has been shown through cell line [91] and small scale studies in animals. Animal data suggest that GQDs are rapidly excreted after being administered through intravenous, intramuscular or subcutaneous routes [183].

GQDs are taken up by living cells and do not lose their fluorescence in a complex physiological environment. Hence, GQD-based probes can be employed to unravel the information at cellular and sub-cellular level [79]. Their affinity for cancer cells can be improved by conjugation with carbohydrate-binding proteins [184], short peptides [185,186], antibody fragments [187] and other recognizable molecules [15,188]. Interestingly, fluorescence properties of GQDs are retained during the conjugation process. In the study reported by Lim and co-workers [187], GQDs showed strong fluorescence in MDA-MB-231 cells after conjugation with a single-chain variable fragment of an antibody (scFv). Their internalization was facilitated through the interaction of functionalized GQDs with the epidermal growth factor receptor being overexpressed by the cells in question.

Negative charge on the plasma membrane facilitates the translocation of cationized carriers. To exploit this property, cationization of carriers is usually carried out by attaching PEI residues available commercially in different molecular weights. Despite its cytotoxicity [189], PEI attachment remains a widely tested approach for the imaging of intracellular organelles [162,190]. Contrary to this notion, Yao et al. [191] illustrated the transport of negatively charged GQDs to the nucleus of tumor cells utilizing their high interstitial fluid pressure (IFP). Insertion of QDs was induced during water-bath heating and laser irradiation. Interestingly, this treatment did not cause any acute toxicity to the cells. The difference in nature of diffusion between differently charged GQDs was explained with molecular dynamics simulations. Authors argued that positively charged particles tend to draw a layer of phospholipid molecules during detachment from the membrane surface, as a result of which these become bulkier and remain localized in the cytosol. On the other hand, negatively charged GQDs electrostatically repel the phospholipid residues and exhibit nuclear targeting [191]. Recently, Dai and coworkers [186] demonstrated the induction of apoptosis in cancer cells once GQDs enter the nucleus ( Fig. 9). These adsorb on single-stranded and supercoiled DNA via π–π and electrostatic interactions. Besides, it was shown that TAT peptide-functionalized dots display superior uptake and accumulation inside the nucleus. Translocation was facilitated by import receptors, importin α and β present over the nuclear membrane.

Multimodal imaging platforms can be developed by coupling GQDs with metal nanoparticles [192,193]. This is verifiable from a study on GQD-Fe3O4 based nanoprobe. In comparison to the control, HeLa cells showed superior contrast upon incubation with folic acid decorated nanoprobe. Its translocation could be attested from the fluorescence in the boundary region of cells even following several washings. In T2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging, a linear relationship was recorded between relaxation rates of resonance signals and iron concentrations [194]. Such a high translocation ability of GQDs can be applied for monitoring the redox homeostasis of cells in a real time manner. Here, fluorescence-switchability can be achieved by functionalization with desferrioxamine B (DFOB), an iron chelator. The design features allowed detection of reactive oxygen species by GQD-DFOB-Fe2+ (PL quenching) and antioxidants by GQD-DFOB-Fe3+ (PL recovery) at physiologically relevant redox changes. A trace amount of H2O2 (30 nM) was detectable with a signal-to-noise ratio of 4.3. Besides, PL quenching displayed a linear relationship with H2O2 concentration [195].

Targeted imaging of tumor mass is essential for its timely diagnosis, staging, and obtaining other desirable anatomical details. In case of deep-seated deposits, imaging demands a high penetration ability of the probe. Imaging should be accomplished without inflicting any radiation damage to the neighbouring tissues [196]. Besides, it is expected that the carriers offer a high signal-to-noise ratio from the target region [197]. Research on photophysical behavior of contrast agents suggests that tissue autofluorescence and out-of-focus signals can be minimized with the use of two-photon excitation modalities [198,199]. High cell permeability, non-blinking fluorescence emission, and quantum confinement effect offer a large two-photon absorption cross-section to GQDs. These properties favor the imaging of thick and deeper structures. Two-photon absorption being facilitated by high intramolecular charge transfer efficiency of π-conjugated domains [200–203], resulting fluorescence can be augmented through the attachment of electron-donating substituents [204]. There is also the possibility of achieving multiplexed labeling of targets through suitable physicochemical modifications over GQD templates.

GQDs have also been employed in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), a powerful non-invasive technique for carrying out clinical examinations. With its 3D imaging features, MRI offers a high spatial resolution of the target [205,206]. MRI performance improves while small-sized hydrophilic GQDs create a modification layer around hydrophobic magnetic nanoparticles (NPs). Here, this layer facilitates capturing and penetration of water towards the contrast agent [207]. It must be noted that the extent of hydrogen bonding between surface/edge hydrophilic groups and surrounding water scales linearly with the enhancement of relaxivity (r1) value [208].

Yang et al. [209] have shown that paramagnetic GQDs offer superior contrast during MRI. Authors developed the platform by incorporating polyethylene glycol (PEG)-functionalized Gd-DOTA (gadolinium-tetraazacyclododecane tetraacetic acid) complex into GQDs. Rotation of Gd3+ could be modulated through the length of PEG. In the presence of hyaluronic acid (HA) as tumor-targeting moiety, intravenously administered PGQDs localized selectively in a small (2.7 mm × 1.8 mm) lung tumor within 2 h and offered high MRI contrast ( Fig. 10A). It must be noted that PEG units prolong the circulation half-life of contrast agents, and thus allow long-term tracking of biological events [196]. Besides, PEG is known to improve the imaging contrast by increasing the longitudinal magnetic relaxivity values. In a mice model, a T1-weighted image showed a brightly lighted tumor region within 30 min post-injection. Tumor area could clearly be distinguished among healthy tissues (Fig. 10B). The authors suggested that r1 increased up to 20-folds in the presence of PEG which accelerated the proton exchange. This effect was further augmented by a localized superacid microenvironment introduced by GQDs [197].

Very recently, Bi and coworkers [210] reported anchoring of single-atom Gd onto GQDs carrying rich oxygen groups. Such a pattern maximizes the utilization of metal and ensures attainment of good contrast at low metal concentration. Gd3+-GQDs self-assembled in aqueous medium as a dendritic structure which offered 25-fold higher relaxivity in comparison to a commercial contrast agent. Another advancement leans over integrating the paramagnetic property of contrast agents with two-photon absorption ability of GQDs. The resulting nanocomposites possess dual-modal imaging functionality which combine the advantages of MRI and fluorescence imaging [207,211].

Tumor microenvironment differs from adjacent healthy tissues in terms of relative expression of specific antigens. These antigens show restricted expression in healthy cells. In case of cancer cells, their expression depends on the stage of differentiation. Therefore, a careful assay of tumor-associated antigens in biopsy specimens can help to identify and delineate the progression of cancer. The data can also be used for monitoring the outcomes of anticancer treatment and post-surgery surveillance. Commonly used antigens include carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), prostate-specific antigen (PSA), and membrane-bound underglycosylated mucins [3,212,213]. CEA assays are clinically being used for monitoring colorectal, breast, liver, lung, and other cancers of epithelial origin [214–216]. On the other hand, assay of PSA is performed in context to diagnosis of prostate cancer. As a member ofextracellular serine protease of kallikrein family, its low blood concentration (4 ng/mL) in healthy males helps in liquefying the seminal fluid [213,217,218].

The detection of biomarkers can be carried out rapidly using a simple and cost-effective manner utilizing GQDs as chemiluminescence (CL) or electro-chemiluminescence (ECL) reagent. ECL combines the advantages of CL and electrochemical analyses and offers a wide response range and high sensitivity relative to fluorescence-based techniques. It is based on immobilization of GQDs over glassy carbon electrodes typically in association with gold NPs ( Fig. 11). Later, this substrate is charged with recognition elements such as ssDNA/RNA aptamers [219] or antibodies against the biomarker [220,221]. Instead of physical adsorption, some groups have attempted chemical anchoring of antibody units over the electrode [218]. Subsequent to binding, quenching of anodic ECL intensity scales proportionally to the logarithm of biomarker concentration [217]. Quenching occurs because of inhibition of electron transfer upon antigen-antibody interaction over the substrate [216].

It is apparent that the sensitivity of such sensors would lean over the rate of electron transfer between GQD nanoprobe and electrode, and the number of active sites available for antigen binding. Accordingly, research groups have explored doping of dots for augmenting the electron transfer kinetics and alteration in the electrode components for increasing the number density of recognition elements. Exploration of aptamers, a class of synthetic recognition agents, is another dimension of biosensing research [222,223]. Notwithstanding the fact that sensitivity of apta- and immuno-sensors may not vary considerably, the former are attractive because of low production cost, and stability against temperature and pH variations [224].

Ecofriendly synthetic routes, tunable optical properties, chemical inertness and biocompatibility make GQDs highly attractive from the standpoint of biomedical applications. Over the past ten years, researchers have been able to illustrate the potential of GQDs in sensing small molecules (ascorbic acid, glucose, hydrogen peroxide), metal ions, enzymes (alkaline phosphatase, proteases) and biomacromolecules such as hemoglobin. The interpretations are largely derived from changes in fluorescence and electrochemical signals. Similar concepts have been extended for the detection of cancer-related biomarkers. This review expands current understanding on the design of GQDs for diagnostic applications. We have emphasized on GQDs as an alternative to expensive organic fluorophores and heavy metal-based semiconductor QDs. Photoluminescence of GQDs emerges from their hyperconjugated domains and it can be improved via doping with heteroatoms. To our understanding, this strategy may contribute significantly in high contrast imaging and identification of tumor-specific biomarkers. It will be interesting to see their recognition specificity upon functionalization with antibodies or other targeting moieties.

From the viewpoint of biosafety, GQDs are well excreted without eliciting significant toxicological events at the tissue or organ level, unlike heavy metal counterparts. In spite of this, their direct exposure to human beings remains a subject of scrutiny and thus, biosafety must be ascertained with robust clinical data. Accordingly, further research must evaluate the synthetic processes in terms of throughput and consistency of quality attributes. This is relevant in light of the fact that polycrystalline dots offer sub-optimal optical properties. At the same time, emphasis must be placed on identifying the type and level of impurities being introduced with a specific synthetic procedure. Near-infrared excitability of well-characterized GQDs can be exploited for photothermal and photodynamic therapy of cancer.

Vrushti Kansara: Writing – original draft. Rahul Shukla: Writing – review & editing. Swaran Jeet Singh Flora: Writing – review & editing. Pratap Bahadur: Writing – review & editing. Sanjay Tiwari: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.
Communication number of this manuscript is NIPER-R/233.
